segments.heText: אַשְׁרֵי יוֹשְׁבֵי בֵיתֶךָ, עוֹד יְהַלְלוּךָ סֶּלָה.
אַשְׁרֵי הָעָם שֶׁכָּכָה לּוֹ, אַשְׁרֵי הָעָם שֶׁיְיָ אֱלֹהָיו.

segments.enText: [Placeholder] Ashrei opens Minchah — we arrive in the middle of the day, catching our breath and returning to Hashem.

[Placeholder insight] Why do we say Ashrei three times a day? The Gemara teaches it guarantees a share in the World to Come — not because of magic, but because of what consistent gratitude does to a person over time.

segments.heText: אֲדֹנָי שְׂפָתַי תִּפְתָּח, וּפִי יַגִּיד תְּהִלָּתֶךָ.
The Amidah follows — same text as Shacharis from Avos through the concluding brachos.

segments.enText: [Placeholder] Minchah's Amidah is the same eighteen brachos as Shacharis. The Arizal teaches that Minchah is in some ways the most important tefillah of the day — it requires the most intention precisely because it interrupts the workday.

segments.heText: עָלֵינוּ לְשַׁבֵּחַ לַאֲדוֹן הַכֹּל, לָתֵת גְּדֻלָּה לְיוֹצֵר בְּרֵאשִׁית.

segments.enText: [Placeholder] Aleinu closes every tefillah — a reminder that the goal of all our prayer is a world where Hashem's presence is universally recognized.
